questionText: It is claimed that for integers $x$ and $y$, if $xy$ is even then $x$ is even or $y$ is even. Determine whether this statement is true, justifying your answer. Then write down its converse and decide whether the converse is also true. Hence state which symbol, $\Rightarrow$ or $\Leftrightarrow$, correctly links the statements $P:\ xy$ is even and $Q:\ x$ is even or $y$ is even.

steps.description: Write the two statements in clear logical form.
steps.workingLatex: P:\ xy \text{ is even}, \qquad Q:\ x \text{ is even or } y \text{ is even}
steps.explanation: Naming the hypothesis $P$ and the conclusion $Q$ lets us treat the claim as $P \Rightarrow Q$ and test it precisely. Being explicit about the 'or' in $Q$ matters, because attacking it directly would force us to juggle two separate cases.

steps.description: Choose a strategy: prove the claim by its contrapositive.
steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q \ \text{ is equivalent to } \ \lnot Q \Rightarrow \lnot P
steps.explanation: A statement and its contrapositive are always true together, so we may prove whichever is easier. Here $\lnot Q$ collapses to a single clean case, which is simpler than chasing the 'or' inside $Q$.

steps.description: Form the negations using De Morgan's law.
steps.workingLatex: \lnot Q:\ x \text{ is odd and } y \text{ is odd}, \qquad \lnot P:\ xy \text{ is odd}
steps.explanation: Negating 'x even or y even' flips the 'or' to 'and' and each part to its opposite, giving 'x odd and y odd'. The contrapositive to prove is therefore: if both $x$ and $y$ are odd, then $xy$ is odd.

steps.description: Write two odd numbers in algebraic form.
steps.explanation: Every odd integer is one more than an even integer, so $2a+1$ captures any odd number. Turning the words into algebra lets us actually compute the product.
The product is an even quantity plus one, which is exactly the definition of an odd number. So multiplying two odd numbers can never produce an even result.

steps.description: Conclude the contrapositive, hence the original statement.
steps.workingLatex: \lnot Q \Rightarrow \lnot P \ \text{ true} \ \Rightarrow \ P \Rightarrow Q \ \text{ true}
steps.explanation: Having shown that two odds give an odd product, the contrapositive holds, and therefore the original statement $P \Rightarrow Q$ is true as well.

steps.workingLatex: \text{Converse } Q \Rightarrow P:\ (x \text{ even or } y \text{ even}) \Rightarrow xy \text{ even}
steps.explanation: The converse simply swaps hypothesis and conclusion. The truth of a statement tells us nothing automatically about its converse, so it must be checked separately.

steps.description: Test the converse assuming x is even.
steps.explanation: If $x$ is even it carries a factor of 2, and multiplying by any integer $y$ keeps that factor, so the product is even. The case where $y$ is even works identically by symmetry.
A single even factor is enough to force the whole product to be even, so the converse holds in every case.

steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q \ \text{ and } \ Q \Rightarrow P \ \Rightarrow \ P \Leftrightarrow Q
steps.explanation: When a statement and its converse are both true, the two statements are logically equivalent, which we record with the two-way symbol $\Leftrightarrow$.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: The statement is true (proved via its contrapositive: two odds multiply to an odd), and its converse is also true, so $P \Leftrightarrow Q$: $xy$ is even if and only if $x$ is even or $y$ is even.
